Category.title: Category
Category.description: Represents a classification for blog posts, helping to organize content by topic.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Category entity.

description.description: A brief description of what this category covers.

Author.title: Author
Author.description: Represents an author who writes blog posts for the FashionGirl Blog.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Author entity.

name.description: The full name of the author.

bio.description: A short biographical sketch of the author.

profileImageUrl.description: URL to the author's profile picture.

BlogPost.title: BlogPost
BlogPost.description: Represents a single blog post published on the FashionGirl Blog, containing its content and metadata.

id.description: Unique identifier for the BlogPost entity.

title.description: The main title of the blog post.

slug.description: A URL-friendly identifier for the blog post, derived from the title.

featuredImageUrl.description: The URL of the main image displayed prominently with the blog post (e.g., in carousels, listings).

excerpt.description: A short summary or snippet of the blog post content, used for listings and previews.

status.description: The current publication status of the blog post ('draft' or 'published').

publishDate.description: The date and time when the blog post was first published.

lastModifiedDate.description: The date and time when the blog post was last updated.

readTimeMinutes.description: The estimated reading time for the blog post in minutes.

authorId.description: Reference to the Author who wrote this blog post. (Relationship: Author 1:N BlogPost)

categoryId.description: Reference to the primary Category this blog post belongs to. (Relationship: Category 1:N BlogPost)

contentBlocks.description: An ordered list of content blocks that compose the body of the blog post, allowing for rich media and text.

ContentBlock.title: ContentBlock
ContentBlock.description: Represents a modular unit of content within a blog post, such as a paragraph, heading, image, video, quote, or a list of links.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ContentBlock entity.

blogPostId.description: Reference to the BlogPost this content block belongs to. (Relationship: BlogPost 1:N ContentBlock)

order.description: The sequential order of this content block within the blog post content.

type.description: The specific type of content block (e.g., 'heading', 'paragraph', 'image', 'video', 'quote', 'links').

fontStyle.description: The specific font style applied to this content block, affecting its rendering on the frontend ('helvetica' for modern, 'quandco' for classic).

textValue.description: The textual content for text-based blocks (heading, subtitle, paragraph, quote).

mediaUrl.description: The URL for image or video blocks. This could be an image URL or a video embed URL (e.g., YouTube/Vimeo).

linkItems.description: An array of link objects for 'links' type content blocks, each containing a label and a URL.

LinkItem.title: LinkItem
LinkItem.description: Represents a single link with a display label and a URL, typically used within a 'links' content block.

id.description: Unique identifier for the LinkItem entity.

contentBlockId.description: Reference to the ContentBlock this link item belongs to. (Relationship: ContentBlock 1:N LinkItem)

label.description: The display text for the link.

url.description: The URL the link points to.

definition.description: Stores user IDs of administrators. The existence of a document in this collection grants administrative privileges within the application. Access is strictly controlled by request.auth.uid for DBAC.
Manages blog post categories. Publicly readable for displaying on the frontend; only administrators can create, update, or delete categories.

params.name: categoryId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific blog category.

definition.description: Stores information about authors of blog posts. Publicly readable to display author profiles; only administrators can create, update, or delete author details.

params.name: authorId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific author.

definition.description: Contains all blog posts, including drafts and published content. Publicly readable only if 'status' is 'published'. Full read/write/delete access is restricted to administrators. Includes denormalized 'authorId' and 'categoryId' for efficient authorization and querying.

params.name: blogPostId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific blog post.

definition.description: A subcollection for each blog post, holding its structured content blocks (e.g., paragraphs, images, quotes). Read access depends on the parent blog post's 'status' (must be 'published' for public reads); full management is restricted to administrators.

params.name: blogPostId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent blog post.

params.name: contentBlockId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific content block within the blog post.

definition.description: A sub-subcollection for link items, specifically used within 'links' type content blocks. Access is inherited from its parent content block and ultimately the blog post (public read if post is published; admin for management).

params.name: blogPostId
params.description: The unique identifier of the grandparent blog post.

params.name: contentBlockId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent content block.

params.name: linkItemId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific link item within the content block.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure for the FashionGirl Blog adheres to the core design principles by emphasizing Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, and consistent Access Modeling. Administrative users are managed via a dedicated `/admin_users/{userId}` collection, leveraging 'Existence over Content' for role-based access control (DBAC). All public-facing entities like `/categories/{categoryId}` and `/authors/{authorId}` are designed to be globally readable, simplifying public access rules.

For blog posts, the `/blog_posts/{blogPostId}` collection stores all posts, regardless of their publication status. Authorization independence for blog posts is achieved by storing critical authorization data directly within each `BlogPost` document, such as `status` (e.g., 'draft', 'published') and `authorId`. This eliminates the need for `get()` calls to other collections when determining read/write access for a specific post. For instance, public read access can be granted if `resource.data.status == 'published'`, while admin write access checks `isAdmin()`, all without cross-document lookups for the main `BlogPost` document.

Subcollections, such as `/blog_posts/{blogPostId}/content_blocks/{contentBlockId}` and `/blog_posts/{blogPostId}/content_blocks/{contentBlockId}/link_items/{linkItemId}`, maintain a hierarchical structure. While these subcollections inherently depend on their parent `BlogPost` for context, access rules are designed to minimize `get()` calls. For public reads of these nested content items, a single `get()` to the immediate parent `blog_posts/{blogPostId}` is used to check its `status` (e.g., if the parent post is 'published'). For admin operations (create, update, delete) on content blocks or link items, the `isAdmin()` function is used. This approach provides a balance between extreme denormalization (copying post status/author into *every* content block/link item) and overly complex, multi-level `get()` dependencies, which can be expensive and hinder atomic operations. The use of a single `get()` to the immediate parent for status inheritance is a widely accepted and practical pattern in Firestore, preserving the spirit of Authorization Independence by avoiding complex, non-local lookups.

QAPs (Query as s Pipelined Security Filter) are supported through structural segregation and explicit status fields. For public users, queries against `/blog_posts` would include a filter like `where('status', '==', 'published')`. The security rules would then enforce that `list` operations only return documents where `status` is 'published' for unauthenticated users, effectively making the rules the 'pipelines' for data access rather than filters applied after retrieval. All documents in public collections (`/categories`, `/authors`) implicitly pass QAPs as they are intended for full public read access. Admin users, identified by their presence in `/admin_users`, have broader `list` and `get` access across all collections and statuses, ensuring that the CMS functionality is fully supported while maintaining strict public data integrity.
